primary_factors: Temperatures remain well above freezing through the bus window (mid/upper 30s °F); no snow/ice event indicated
Multiple sources agree precip type is rain/drizzle with 0" snowfall; no winter-weather alerts (only Dense Fog Advisory)
Primary operational hazard is dense fog/near-zero visibility roughly 2:00–8:30 AM, which more often drives delays than full closures in West Michigan
No confirmed Rockford or neighboring-district closure/delay announcements; no community ‘closure buzz’ (no domino-effect pressure)
Power outages appear minimal/decreasing countywide and are unlikely to force a district-wide closure
decision_rationale: All experts converge on a non-snow, above-freezing ‘warm-sector’ setup: rain/drizzle, saturated air, and dense fog. That removes the main Michigan closure drivers (accumulating snow, widespread ice, blizzard winds, or extreme wind chill). The only credible pathway to a full closure is if fog verifies as widespread near-zero visibility right during 6–8 AM across most of Rockford’s rural routes, potentially combined with a small number of slick bridge/overpass spots. Historically and operationally, districts usually manage that with slower routes or a 1–2 hour delay rather than canceling outright. News intelligence shows no confirmed closure/delay announcements from Rockford or nearby districts and no community buzz—so there’s no ‘domino effect’ pressure pushing administrators toward closure. Power-grid conditions are not a meaningful driver (outages appear minimal/decreasing). Net: low but non-zero closure risk; meaningful chance of a delay if dense fog is observed at decision time.

collaboration.summary: Agents reached consensus after 2 round(s) with 7.0% spread. Average probability: 7%.
